title: Guilt Is Not the Measure of Good Mothering
subtitle: An affirmation for separating mothering quality from the volume of guilt carried
excerpt: A short affirmation for breaking the equation between guilt and care. For the mother who believes that the amount of guilt she carries is proof of how much she loves her children.

content.text: A short affirmation for separating guilt from quality of care. There is a common belief that the more a mother worries and feels guilty, the better she loves. This equation keeps guilt in place as proof of virtue and makes reducing guilt feel like caring less. This affirmation breaks that link.
Say it three times, slowly. Hand on your heart if it helps.
Guilt is not the measure of good mothering. My love is not proved by my suffering.

The first sentence states a fact about the relationship between guilt and quality of care. The second removes the mechanism: love is not demonstrated by suffering. It is demonstrated by presence, attention, and showing up, none of which require guilt as proof.
